serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of chilli prawn is approximately 250 g (one plate), providing around 580 kcal, 35 g protein, 40 g fat, and 20 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Shrimp, cooked, breaded and fried (FDC ID 175180) and Shrimp, mixed species, cooked, moist heat (FDC ID 175177), cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient composition data for fried seafood dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Indian-style chilli prawn exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Foodo. Values are adapted from USDA FDC fried/breaded shrimp (FDC ID 175180) for the deep-fried prawn base, adjusted for the Indian-style sauce components (onion, capsicum, chilli, tomato paste, soy sauce) using USDA SR Legacy ingredient data. Sodium elevated to 480 mg/100g reflecting soy sauce and salt additions. Input values from ai_generated record (confidence 0.7) are consistent with this adapted estimate and are retained with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Indian-style chilli prawn. GI estimated at 50 (low) based on analogous mixed fried seafood dish with a light starch-based coating and vegetable sauce components. The carbohydrate content is low (8 g/100g), primarily from a thin cornflour coating and tomato-based sauce; the dominant macronutrient is protein (14 g/100g) and fat (16 g/100g), both of which suppress glycaemic response. GI ≤55 is classified as low per the University of Sydney GI Database standard. GL per serving calculated as: GI (50) × carbohydrate per serving (8 g/100g × 250 g = 20 g) / 100 = 10.0. Analogous GI anchor: battered/breaded fried fish and mixed seafood dishes with low carbohydrate load, GI approximately 45–55 per University of Sydney GI Database.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 480 mg/100g; a 250 g serving delivers 1200 mg sodium, representing approximately 52% of the WHO daily limit of 2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion to 80–100 g, request preparation with reduced soy sauce and no added salt, and avoid this dish on days when other high-sodium foods are consumed.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate at 8 g/100g with low GI (~50); high protein (14 g/100g) from tiger prawns supports glycaemic control, but deep-frying adds significant fat and the cornflour coating contributes refined starch.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes may consume a moderate portion (100–150 g) of chilli prawn, preferably prepared by stir-frying without prior deep-frying and with minimal cornflour coating, to limit refined starch and excess fat intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying elevates total fat to 16 g/100g with saturated fat 3.2 g/100g and trans fat 0.2 g/100g; cholesterol is 140 mg/100g; sodium at 480 mg/100g further burdens cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid the deep-fried preparation and request stir-fried prawn with minimal oil, reduced soy sauce, and no added salt, limiting portion to 80–100 g to manage combined sodium, saturated fat, and cholesterol load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; nightshade ingredients (capsicum, green chillies) may trigger inflammatory responses in susceptible individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid the deep-fried preparation entirely and request stir-fried prawn without nightshade vegetables, as AGE formation and nightshade compounds may worsen joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chilli Prawn (also spelled Chilly Prawn or Chili Prawn) is a popular Indian-Singaporean seafood dish featuring tiger prawns that are deep-fried until crisp and then stir-fried in a wok or kadai with sliced onions, capsicum, green chillies, tomato paste, soy sauce, ginger, and turmeric to create a spicy, tangy, and aromatic sauce. The dish is characterised by its bold heat from green chillies, the umami depth of soy sauce, and the natural sweetness of capsicum, producing a complex flavour profile that is simultaneously spicy, savoury, and slightly sweet. The texture contrasts the crisp exterior of the deep-fried prawn with the tender, juicy flesh inside, coated in a thick, glossy sauce. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 232 kcal, 14 g protein, 16 g fat (including 3.2 g saturated fat and 0.2 g trans fat from deep-frying), and 8 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 480 mg reflecting soy sauce and salt additions. Cholesterol is elevated at 140 mg/100g due to the prawn content. The dish is halal and dairy-free but contains crustacean and soy allergens. Clinically, the high sodium content (480 mg/100g) is a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients, while the low carbohydrate load and low GI (~50) make it relatively suitable for type 2 diabetes management when portion-controlled and prepared without deep-frying. The dish is commonly served as a main course in Indian restaurants and hawker centres across Singapore, typically accompanied by roti prata, naan, or steamed rice.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chilli prawn?
Is chilli prawn high in sodium?
What is the difference between chilli prawn and chilli crab?

confidence_reason: No exact match for Indian-style chilli prawn exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FDC fried/breaded shrimp data cross-referenced with SR Legacy ingredient data for sauce components. The input ai_generated values (confidence 0.7) are consistent with this adapted estimate. GI is an analogous estimate. Medium confidence is appropriate.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this Indian-style deep-fried chilli prawn dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo as of 2026-04-16. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was reviewed but does not provide a matching entry for this specific dish. All core macros (protein_g 14.0, fat_g 16.0, carbohydrate_g 8.0) are non-zero and supported by adapted USDA FDC data; no zero-macro justification is required. Trans fat 0.2 g/100g is a low but non-zero value consistent with deep-frying in commercial oil. Ethnic tags in the input (Curry, Biryani, Naan) are not applicable to this dish and have been removed from the output. The dish is classified as Indian-Singaporean cuisine reflecting its Indian culinary roots and Singaporean context of origin. GI revised to 50 (low) from prior draft value of 55 to correctly reflect the low-GI classification threshold (GI ≤55 = low per University of Sydney GI Database standard); gi_category corrected to 'low' accordingly; GL per serving recalculated to 10.0.
